examples.title: Simple Usability Heuristic Evaluation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm for conducting heuristic evaluation on an interface
examples.code:
BEGIN
 SET heuristics = ["visibility", "match", "control", "consistency", 
 "error_prevention", "recognition", "flexibility", 
 "minimalist", "error_recovery", "help"]
 SET issues = EMPTY_LIST
 
 FOR each evaluator IN evaluators:
 FOR each screen IN interface:
 FOR each heuristic IN heuristics:
 issue = evaluate_against_heuristic(screen, heuristic)
 IF issue EXISTS:
 issues.APPEND(issue)
 
 severity_scores = rate_severity(issues)
 sort_by_severity(issues, severity_scores)
 
 RETURN top_issues for redesign
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how heuristic evaluation works - multiple evaluators systematically check each interface element against established usability principles, then rate issues by severity for prioritized fixes
examples.timeComplexity: O(e × s × h) where e=evaluators, s=screens, h=heuristics

examples.title: User Task Effectiveness Calculation
examples.description: Calculate effectiveness metric for usability testing
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION calculate_effectiveness(participants, tasks):
 results = []
 
 FOR each participant IN participants:
 FOR each task IN tasks:
 completed = task.attempted AND task.successful
 completion_rate = (completed / total_attempts) × 100
 
 result = {
 participant_id: participant.id,
 task_id: task.id,
 completed: completed,
 time_taken: task.end_time - task.start_time,
 errors: count_errors(task)
 }
 results.APPEND(result)
 
 avg_completion = AVERAGE(results.completion_rate)
 avg_time = AVERAGE(results.time_taken)
 avg_errors = AVERAGE(results.errors)
 
 RETURN {
 effectiveness_score: avg_completion,
 efficiency_metric: avg_time,
 error_rate: avg_errors,
 recommendation: determine_recommendation(avg_completion)
 }
 
 OUTPUT = calculate_effectiveness(test_participants, test_tasks)
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm processes usability test data to calculate effectiveness (task completion), efficiency (time taken), and error rates - key metrics for measuring usability according to ISO 9241-11

examples.title: User Journey Touchpoint Analysis
examples.description: Process user journey data to identify pain points and opportunities
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION analyze_journey(journey_steps, user_emotions):
 touchpoints = []
 pain_points = []
 opportunities = []
 
 FOR each step IN journey_steps:
 emotion_at_step = user_emotions[step.id]
 
 touchpoint = {
 channel: step.channel,
 action: step.action,
 emotion_score: emotion_at_step.score,
 satisfaction: emotion_at_step.satisfaction_rating
 }
 touchpoints.APPEND(touchpoint)
 
 IF emotion_at_step.score < THRESHOLD_LOW:
 pain_point = {
 step: step.name,
 issue: identify_issue(emotion_at_step),
 severity: calculate_severity(emotion_at_step)
 }
 pain_points.APPEND(pain_point)
 
 opportunity = suggest_improvement(step, pain_point)
 opportunities.APPEND(opportunity)
 
 RETURN {
 journey_map: touchpoints,
 critical_pain_points: sort_by_severity(pain_points),
 recommended_improvements: opportunities
 }
 
 OUTPUT = analyze_journey(checkout_journey, emotion_data)
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode processes user journey data, identifying where users experience negative emotions (pain points) and generating improvement opportunities - essential for experience optimization
examples.timeComplexity: O(s) where s=number of journey steps
examples.spaceComplexity: O(s + p) for touchpoints and pain points storage
